title: Saint Louis vs Michigan — Round of 32 Viewing Guide
eventTag: NCAA Tournament • Round of 32 • Buffalo, NY
venue: KeyBank Center, Buffalo, NY • 5:15 PM ET • CBS
extraInfo: Winner advances to the Sweet 16 in Los Angeles

stakes.callout: The most fun team in America meets the best defense in America — and both just made a statement. Saint Louis demolished Georgia 102-77, shooting 58% and going on a 23-0 run that buried the Bulldogs. Michigan handled Howard 101-80, but allowed 14 three-pointers and Lendeborg looked limited (9 points on a healing ankle). SLU has four 40%+ shooters. Michigan has the #1 defense in KenPom. Something has to break.

cards.label: For Saint Louis
cards.value: Just demolished Georgia 102-77 — shooting 58.3% and scoring 66 points in the paint. Eight players scored 7+, the bench dropped 43 points. The late-season wobble (3-4 in their last 7) looks like ancient history. A Sweet 16 would be SLU's first since 2014 and validate one of the best coaching jobs in the country.

cards.label: For Michigan
cards.value: Beat Howard 101-80, but allowed 14 three-pointers — the most concerning stat for a team about to face the 2nd-best three-point shooting team in America. Morez Johnson went 8-for-8 (21 points), but Lendeborg scored only 9 on a healing ankle. Michigan needs its best player at full strength against SLU's shooting barrage.

cards.label: The Shooting Test
cards.value: SLU shoots 40.1% from three (2nd nationally) with four 40%+ shooters. Michigan holds opponents to one of the lowest shooting percentages in the country. This is the unstoppable force vs. immovable object matchup of the tournament.

cards.label: The Lendeborg Watch
cards.value: Michigan's Big Ten Player of the Year scored only 9 points against Howard on a healing ankle. He played 28 minutes but didn't look like himself. Against SLU's pace and shooting, a limited Lendeborg is a real vulnerability — and SLU will attack wherever they sense weakness.

styles.title: Shoot the Lights Out, Share the Ball
bullets: Historic shooting: 40.1% from three (2nd nationally) with a school-record 360 made threes. Four players shoot 40%+: Green (45.7%), Sharma (42.8%), Avila (41.6%), Brown (40.4%). This is the deepest shooting team in the tournament.

Pace and movement: SLU plays fast (21st in adjusted tempo) and moves the ball unselfishly (6th nationally in assists). Eight players average 5+ PPG. They score 87.2 PPG (10th nationally) on 51.0% FG (5th nationally).

Avila is a unicorn: Robbie Avila is a 6-10 center who leads all D-I centers in assists (4.1/game) and three-pointers (1.9/game). He stretches defenses in ways most big men can't. If Mara guards him, Avila pulls him to the perimeter. If someone else guards him, he posts up.
Defense is real too: Opponents shoot just 35.8% against SLU (1st nationally) and 26.6% from three. KenPom 22nd in defensive efficiency. This isn't a one-dimensional shooting team.

First-round statement: SLU demolished Georgia 102-77 — shooting 58.3% with a 23-0 run. Dion Brown went 9-of-10. Eight players scored 7+. The bench dropped 43 points. The late-season wobble (3-4 in 7) is officially dead. This team is rolling.

styles.title: The Best Defense in America
bullets: #1 defense: KenPom adjusted defensive efficiency: No. 1 nationally. Anchored by 7-3 Aday Mara (Big Ten DPOY). Opponents shoot .387 against Michigan. Length, switching, and rim protection suffocate offenses.

Elite two-way team: KenPom #2 overall, #1 defense, #8 offense. Score 86.8 PPG while playing elite defense. SRS of 32.48 is 1st nationally. One of the best two-way teams in modern college basketball history.
Road dominance: Michigan went 10-0 in Big Ten road games. They don't need home-court advantage. This translates to neutral-site composure in the tournament.

Portal-built depth: Lendeborg (UAB), Mara (UCLA), Cadeau (UNC), Johnson (Illinois) — May rebuilt the roster through elite transfers. Burnett, a 6th-year senior, dropped 31 vs. Penn State. This team is deep.

First-round red flags: Beat Howard 101-80 but allowed 14 three-pointers — a season high. Lendeborg scored only 9 on a bum ankle. L.J. Cason's torn ACL limits backcourt depth. If SLU's 40%+ shooters get the same looks Howard's 33% shooters got, this could be a problem.

keys.title: Michigan allowed 14 threes to Howard — SLU shoots better.
keys.description: The biggest red flag from Michigan's first-round win: Howard hit 14 three-pointers, the most Michigan has allowed all season. Howard shoots 33% from three. SLU shoots 40.1% — 2nd nationally — with four players over 40%. If a 16-seed can exploit Michigan's perimeter defense, what can the best shooting team in the tournament do? This is the matchup that keeps Dusty May up at night.

keys.title: The Avila-Mara chess match.
keys.description: The most fascinating matchup in the Round of 32. Avila is a 6-10 center who shoots 41.6% from three and leads all D-I centers in assists. Mara is 7-3, the Big Ten DPOY, and changes every shot near the rim. If Avila pulls Mara to the perimeter, Michigan loses its rim protector. If Mara stays home, Avila kicks to four shooters who all shoot 40%+. May has to choose his poison.

keys.title: SLU's pace vs. Michigan's length.
keys.description: SLU plays at the 21st-fastest tempo in America. Michigan's defense is built on length and switching, which can slow teams down. If SLU can push pace, create transition threes, and force Michigan into a track meet, the defense has less time to set up. If Michigan controls tempo and makes SLU grind in the half-court against their length, the shooting windows close.

keys.title: Lendeborg scored 9 against Howard. Can Michigan survive that again?
keys.description: The Big Ten Player of the Year had just 9 points in 28 minutes against Howard — clearly not himself on the healing ankle. Michigan won because Johnson went 8-for-8 and Mara had 19/7/6/3 blocks. But SLU is a different animal. If Lendeborg is limited again, Michigan needs Johnson and Mara to repeat their heroics against a team that holds opponents to the lowest FG% in America. That's a much harder ask.

keys.title: SLU's 23-0 run buried Georgia. Can they do it to Michigan?
keys.description: The late-season wobble is officially over. SLU went on a 23-0 run spanning halftime against Georgia, turning a competitive game into a 102-77 rout. They shot 58.3%, scored 66 points in the paint, and got 43 bench points. This is the 24-1 version of Saint Louis — confident, deep, and clicking. The question is whether that translates against the best defense in America or whether Michigan's length turns a 23-0 run into a 23-0 memory.

prediction.summary: The Avila-Mara chess match defines everything. If Avila pulls the 7-3 Mara out to the three-point line, Michigan loses its rim protector and SLU's four 40%+ shooters feast inside. If Mara stays home, Avila — shooting 41.6% from deep — punishes Michigan from the perimeter and kicks to Green, Sharma, and Brown. Michigan allowed 14 threes to Howard, a 33% shooting team. SLU shoots 40.1%. That's the math that keeps Dusty May up at night. The counterargument: Lendeborg's health. If the Big Ten Player of the Year is truly limited — 9 points against Howard suggests he might be — Michigan needs Johnson and Mara to carry the load again. SLU's defense (opponents shoot 35.8%, best nationally) is also elite, so this isn't just a shootout. It's a battle between the two best defenses either team has faced all year.

phases.title: Opening 10 Minutes
phases.description: Michigan's defense clamps down early, contesting SLU's three-point looks and forcing Avila into tougher decisions. Mara's length bothers everything near the rim. SLU starts 1-of-5 from three. But Michigan's own offense sputters without a healthy Lendeborg, and the game starts in a 12-10 rock fight.

phases.title: Mid-First Half to Halftime
phases.description: Trey Green heats up. He hits two pull-up threes in a 90-second span, and suddenly SLU's confidence is back. Avila finds Brown cutting to the basket off Mara's over-help. Michigan responds with Johnson attacking inside and Cadeau pushing pace. Halftime is tight — Michigan leads 31-29, but SLU is shooting better from deep and the Billikens' bench is keeping legs fresh.

phases.title: Second Half — SLU Makes Its Run
phases.description: The 23-0 run that buried Georgia? SLU has another one in them. Green, Avila, and Sharma hit three consecutive threes in a 4-minute stretch, and the Billikens take a 5-point lead. Michigan's perimeter defense, exposed against Howard, is struggling again. May calls timeout and switches to a zone. SLU's ball movement picks it apart.

phases.title: Final 8 Minutes
phases.description: Michigan's championship DNA kicks in. Johnson and Mara dominate inside, and Cadeau finds driving lanes against SLU's scrambling defense. The Wolverines erase the deficit and take a 1-point lead with 3 minutes left. It comes down to execution — and Michigan's road-tested composure (10-0 in Big Ten road games) gives them the edge in a possession-by-possession finish.

projectedScore.rationale: Michigan's defensive identity and clutch composure survive SLU's shooting barrage in a thriller, but the Billikens prove they belong — Avila and Green combine for 35+.

players.name: Robbie Avila
players.meta: C • 6-10 • Junior • A-10 Player of the Year • "Cream Abdul-Jabbar"
players.bio:
From Oak Forest, IL. Leads all D-I centers in assists/game (4.1) and three-pointers/game (1.9). Third center in NCAA history with 200+ career three-pointers. Wears protective goggles that spawned the nicknames "Cream Abdul-Jabbar," "Larry Nerd," "Steph Blurry," and "Milk Chamberlain." Followed coach Schertz from Indiana State. The Defector described him as looking "like he should be digging through record crates."
Read more →

players.name: Trey Green
players.bio: From Charlotte, NC. Xavier transfer (BIG EAST All-Freshman Team). One of the most lethal shooters in the nation at 45.7% from three on 90 makes. Made seven threes in one game against Dayton. His spacing alongside Avila creates enormous offensive pressure — if Michigan helps off Green, he'll make them pay.

players.name: Dion Brown
players.bio: From Great Barrington, MA. Path: UMBC → Boston College → SLU. Described as "positively overflowing with dawg" by The Defector. On team ethos: "It doesn't matter who hit it...Robbie didn't hit the shot. We hit it." Shoots 60.6% from the field and 40.4% from three — efficiency from everywhere.

players.name: Ishan Sharma
players.meta: G • 6-5 • Sophomore • Virginia Transfer • Ontario, Canada
players.bio: From Milton, Ontario, Canada. Virginia transfer. Fourth SLU player shooting 40%+ from deep — when four guys shoot like this, the floor spacing is nearly impossible to guard. Season-high 29 points at St. Bonaventure. Part of what makes SLU so dangerous: there's no one to leave open.

players.name: Yaxel Lendeborg
players.stats: 14.6 PPG • Big Ten Player of the Year
players.meta: G/F • Puerto Rico → JUCO → UAB → Michigan
players.bio:
One of the most remarkable stories in college basketball. Grew up in Puerto Rico. Cut from his high school team for bad grades. Played only 11 total high school games. Went JUCO, then UAB, then Michigan. Now the Big Ten Player of the Year who turned down $7-9 million from Kentucky to chase this. Sprained his ankle in the Big Ten Tournament championship — his mobility is the X-factor.
Read more →

players.name: Aday Mara
players.stats: Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year • 7-3
players.meta: C • 7-3 • From Spain • Transfer from UCLA
players.bio: A 7-foot-3 center from Spain who transferred from UCLA and became the Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year. Anchors the #1-ranked defense in America. His matchup with Avila is the chess match of the tournament — does he follow Avila to the three-point line and abandon the paint, or stay home and give a 41.6% shooter open looks?

players.name: Morez Johnson Jr.
players.bio: Illinois Mr. Basketball who transferred from Illinois to Michigan. A versatile, physical forward who gives Michigan elite production on both ends. One of the key pieces in the transformation from 8-24 to 32-3. Provides the kind of secondary scoring Michigan needs when facing a team that can keep up offensively.

players.name: Elliot Cadeau
players.bio: Transferred from North Carolina to run Michigan's offense. Averages 5.6 assists per game while providing steady, composed playmaking. His ball security and decision-making will be tested by SLU's defensive pressure — the Billikens hold opponents to the lowest FG% in America.

funFacts.title: "Cream Abdul-Jabbar" (SLU)
funFacts.body: Robbie Avila wears protective goggles and has earned the nicknames "Cream Abdul-Jabbar," "Larry Nerd," "Steph Blurry," and "Milk Chamberlain" from fans. He leads all D-I centers in assists AND three-pointers. The Defector described him as looking "like he should be digging through record crates."

funFacts.title: Cut from His High School Team (Michigan)
funFacts.body: Yaxel Lendeborg — the Big Ten Player of the Year — was cut from his high school team in Puerto Rico for bad grades. He played only 11 total high school games. He went to JUCO, then UAB, then Michigan. He turned down $7-9 million from Kentucky to stay.

funFacts.title: What IS a Billiken? (SLU)
funFacts.body: A mythical good-luck charm representing "things as they ought to be." Created by Missouri art teacher Florence Pretz in 1908, inspired by the Rubaiyat of Omar Khayyam. SLU is the only university with a Billiken mascot. People rub his belly for good luck. More →

funFacts.title: 8-24 to 32-3 in Two Years (Michigan)
funFacts.body: Michigan went 8-24 in 2023-24 — dead last in the Big Ten. Two years later, they're 32-3 with the #1 defense in America and the #1 seed. Dusty May did it through the transfer portal: Lendeborg (UAB), Mara (UCLA), Cadeau (UNC), Johnson (Illinois).

funFacts.title: From 13-20 to 29-5 (SLU)
funFacts.body: Two years ago, SLU was 13-20 and 13th in the A-10. Josh Schertz was hired, brought Robbie Avila from Indiana State, and went 29-5. His career winning percentage: .773 (8th-best among active NCAA coaches).

funFacts.title: The Fab Five (Michigan)
funFacts.body: Michigan's most iconic basketball legacy: Chris Webber, Jalen Rose, Juwan Howard, Jimmy King, and Ray Jackson arrived in 1991 and reached back-to-back championship games. They revolutionized basketball culture with baggy shorts, black socks, and swagger. Michigan's only title came in 1989.

funFacts.title: NASA's Flight Director (SLU)
funFacts.body: Eugene Kranz — the NASA flight director who managed the Gemini and Apollo missions, including the famous Apollo 13 rescue — is a Saint Louis University alumnus. So is Maxine Clark, founder of Build-A-Bear Workshop.

funFacts.title: 7-3 from Spain (Michigan)
funFacts.body: Aday Mara is a 7-foot-3 center from Spain who transferred from UCLA. He's the Big Ten Defensive Player of the Year and changes the geometry of the paint. His matchup against Avila — a three-point shooting center — is an unstoppable force vs. immovable object scenario.

funFacts.title: First University West of the Mississippi (SLU)
funFacts.body: Founded in 1818, Saint Louis University is the oldest university west of the Mississippi River and the second-oldest Jesuit university in the United States. It predates Missouri statehood by three years.

funFacts.title: Presidents and Google (Michigan)
funFacts.body: Michigan alumni include Gerald Ford (38th President), Tom Brady (7 Super Bowls), Larry Page (co-founder of Google), and Arthur Miller (Pulitzer Prize playwright). Madonna attended but didn't graduate.

funFacts.title: The FAU Miracle (Michigan's Coach)
funFacts.body: Before Michigan, Dusty May coached Florida Atlantic to the Final Four in 2023 — one of the greatest Cinderella runs in tournament history. Now he's trying to take a #1 seed to the same destination. Two very different paths, same goal.

funFacts.title: The Canadian Sniper (SLU)
funFacts.body: Ishan Sharma from Milton, Ontario, transferred from Virginia and shoots 42.8% from three. He's the fourth Billiken shooting over 40% from deep — making SLU perhaps the only team in America where the fourth-best shooter is still a 42% marksman.

history.callout: Two programs with very different tournament pedigrees. Michigan has a national championship (1989), the Fab Five's cultural revolution, and John Beilein's back-to-back title game runs. Saint Louis is a mid-major underdog that reached the Sweet 16 in 2014 and hasn't been back since. But Cinderella stories are what make March — and SLU has the shooting to write one.

timeline.text: Michigan's only national championship, won under interim coach Steve Fisher. Beat Seton Hall in the title game.

Chris Webber, Jalen Rose, Juwan Howard, Jimmy King, and Ray Jackson reached back-to-back championship games as freshmen and sophomores. Revolutionized basketball culture. Later vacated due to NCAA violations.
John Beilein reached the national championship game twice — losing to Louisville in 2013 and Villanova in 2018. Built Michigan into a consistent contender.
Michigan went 8-24, last in the Big Ten. The program appeared dead. Then they hired Dusty May.
SLU won the NIT championship when it was considered a more prestigious tournament than the NCAA Tournament.
Under Jim Crews, SLU knocked off NC State in the Round of 32 to reach the Sweet 16. Lost to Louisville. The last time the Billikens advanced this far.
Josh Schertz arrived from Indiana State with Robbie Avila and a .773 career winning percentage. Year 1: 19-15. Year 2: 29-5 and back in the tournament.

subsections.title: The Dusty May Revolution
subsections.content: What Dusty May has done at Michigan may be the greatest turnaround in college basketball history. In 2023-24, the Wolverines went 8-24 — dead last in the Big Ten. May, fresh off taking Florida Atlantic to the Final Four, rebuilt through the transfer portal: Yaxel Lendeborg from UAB, Aday Mara from UCLA, Elliot Cadeau from UNC, Morez Johnson from Illinois. Two years later: 32-3, #1 seed, the best defense in America. The 24-win improvement in one season is staggering.
